title: Database Management
description: Core database operations, creation, and configuration

concepts.title: Creating Databases

sqlServer.code:
-- Basic database creation
CREATE DATABASE SampleDB;

-- Database with custom settings
CREATE DATABASE SampleDB_Custom
ON
( NAME = 'SampleDB_Data',
  FILENAME = 'C:\Data\SampleDB.mdf',
  SIZE = 100MB,
  MAXSIZE = 1GB,
  FILEGROWTH = 10MB )
LOG ON
( NAME = 'SampleDB_Log',
  FILENAME = 'C:\Logs\SampleDB.ldf',
  SIZE = 10MB,
  FILEGROWTH = 10% );

-- Database with collation
CREATE DATABASE SampleDB_Collated
COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS;

-- Drop database
DROP DATABASE SampleDB;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server database creation involves specifying file locations, initial sizes, and growth settings. Collation is set at database level. Explicit file path specification required with separate data and log file configuration.

postgres.description: PostgreSQL uses templates for database creation. Templates provide a starting point with predefined objects and settings. template1 is the default template. Can create custom templates from existing databases.

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server requires explicit file path specification with separate data and log files, while PostgreSQL uses template-based creation with no explicit file paths - all file management is handled by PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL supports database cloning via templates.

concepts.title: Database Configuration

sqlServer.description: SQL Server configuration includes database-level settings via ALTER DATABASE and server-level settings via sp_configure. Database options set via ALTER DATABASE, server options require sp_configure and RECONFIGURE. Some settings require SQL Server restart.

postgres.description: PostgreSQL configuration uses postgresql.conf for server settings, pg_hba.conf for authentication, and supports database/role-specific overrides. postgresql.conf contains main configuration. Many settings can be changed without restart.

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server stores configuration in system tables and requires sp_configure/RECONFIGURE for changes, while PostgreSQL uses file-based configuration (postgresql.conf and pg_hba.conf) with hierarchical settings that can be overridden at database, role, or session level. PostgreSQL supports hot reload for many settings.
